%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# The Time-Integration Property

> **Section Objective:** Learn how time-domain integration changes a Laplace transform.

---

**Concepts In This Section**

- integration from \(0^-\)
- division by \(s\)
- integration from \(-\infty\)
- pre-0 accumulated area term

## 1. Integration from 0^-

The expression \(\int_{0^-}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\) means the **running accumulated area** of \(x(t)\), starting just before time zero.

Here \(\tau\) is a dummy variable that sweeps from \(0^-\) up to the current time \(t\). The output at each moment is the total signed area collected so far.

**Minimal example:** If \(x(t)\) is a positive rectangular pulse after zero, the integral output starts at zero and climbs steadily as more positive area is swept in. The output is not the height of \(x(t\)) — it is the growing total.

#### Key point

Because the lower limit is \(0^-\), the accumulated value at the very start equals zero. There is no stored history from before zero in this version.

## 2. Why division by s appears

**Formula (4.16a) — the first core property.**

If \(x(t)\Longleftrightarrow X(s)\), then integrating \(x(t)\) from \(0^-\) to \(t\) **divides** its Laplace transform by \(s\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(\tau\) — dummy integration variable (sweeps from \(0^-\) to \(t\))
- \(t\) — current time (the upper limit)
- \(X(s)\) — Laplace transform of \(x(t)\)
- \(s\) — the Laplace complex variable

**Exam trigger:** Any time integral whose lower limit is \(0^-\) maps to \(X(s)/s\).

**Common misuse:** Writing \(sX(s)\) instead of \(X(s)/s\). Multiplication by \(s\) is the **differentiation** pattern. Integration is the opposite — it divides by \(s\).

$$\int_{0^-}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\Longleftrightarrow \frac{X(s)}{s}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Integration from -\infty

Define \(g(t) = \int_{0^-}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\). By the fundamental theorem of calculus, differentiating \(g(t)\) gives back \(x(t)\):

$$\frac{d}{dt}g(t) = x(t)$$

Now apply the **Laplace differentiation property** to \(g(t)\). That property says the transform of \(\frac{d}{dt}g(t)\) is \(sG(s) - g(0^-)\).

Because the integral starts at \(0^-\), the accumulated value at the start is \(g(0^-) = 0\). So:

$$X(s) = sG(s) \implies G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{s}$$

#### Exam value

This proof reveals exactly why the initial accumulated value matters. If \(g(0^-)\) were not zero — as happens when the lower limit is \(-\infty\) — an extra term would survive and appear in the transform.

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d}{dt}g(t)\right\} = sG(s) - g(0^-)$$
***Local proof formula** — used to derive the integration property, not the main result itself.

Here \(g(t)\Longleftrightarrow G(s)\), and the derivative of \(g(t)\) transforms to \(sG(s)\) minus the left-limit initial value \(g(0^-)\).

**When to use it:** When proving or verifying the time-integration rule from first principles.

**Common mistake:** Forgetting the \(g(0^-)\) term entirely. Students who drop it are then confused about why the \(-\infty\) version of the property has an extra constant term — that extra term is precisely the non-zero \(g(0^-)\) that was ignored.*

## 3. Integration from \(-\infty\)

The expression \(\int_{-\infty}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\) sweeps all the way from the distant past. It naturally splits into two parts:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau = \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau}_{\text{pre-0 stored area}} + \underbrace{\int_{0^-}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau}_{\text{running area from }0^-}$$

**Minimal concrete example:** Suppose the signal had total area \(C = 3\) before time zero. Then at \(t = 0^-\) the integral output already equals 3, not 0. The output does not start from scratch — it begins with that stored value.

#### Key point

For \(t \ge 0\), the pre-0 area \(\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau\) is a **fixed constant** — it does not change with \(t\). The Laplace transform of a constant step contribution is that constant divided by \(s\).

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\Longleftrightarrow \frac{X(s)}{s}+\frac{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau}{s}$$
***Formula (4.16b) — the second core property.**

- **First term** \(X(s)/s\): the same running integration from \(0^-\), identical to formula (4.16a).
- **Second term** \(\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau\,/\,s\): the pre-0 accumulated area, treated as a constant, divided by \(s\).

**Symbol note:** \(\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau\) is a **number** (a constant), not a function of \(t\). Its Laplace transform is that constant over \(s\).

**Exam trigger:** The lower limit is \(-\infty\), or the problem states that the signal has history before zero.

**Common misuse:** Applying only \(X(s)/s\) and dropping the second term. This is wrong whenever the signal has any accumulated area before \(0^-\).*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Integration from \(0^-\): \(\int_{0^-}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\Longleftrightarrow X(s)/s\) — time integration divides the transform by \(s\); differentiation multiplies by \(s\).
- Integration from \(-\infty\): \(\int_{-\infty}^{t}x(\tau)\,d\tau\Longleftrightarrow X(s)/s+\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau\,/\,s\) — the extra term is the pre-0 accumulated area divided by \(s\).
- The extra term \(\int_{-\infty}^{0^-}x(\tau)\,d\tau\,/\,s\) is a constant (a fixed number, not a function of \(t\)); drop it only when the lower limit is \(0^-\).
- Exam trap: multiplying by \(s\) is differentiation; dividing by \(s\) is integration — never swap them.

*Next, use these transform properties to simplify system-analysis problems without recomputing integrals from scratch.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
